title: SEGMENT 15 — Ancient DNA microbiomes: reading the past from teeth and mummies

lines.text: SEGMENT 15 — Ancient DNA microbiomes: reading the past from teeth and mummies

One of the most enchanting and methodologically beautiful subfields of microbiomics is ancient microbiome research — the use of high-throughput sequencing to recover microbial DNA from archaeological remains, sometimes thousands of years old.

The same culture-free DNA sequencing approach that revealed the modern microbiome can be applied to skeletal material, mummified tissue, coprolites (fossilized feces), and other archaeological specimens to ask what the microbiome looked like in the deep past.
The challenge with ancient DNA is, of course, degradation.
DNA in dead tissue breaks down rapidly under most conditions.
After a few centuries, what remains is short, chemically damaged fragments — typically less than 100 base pairs long, with characteristic patterns of cytosine deamination at the ends.
After a few thousand years, only specific preservation conditions allow recovery — cold environments, dry environments, anaerobic environments, or specific kinds of mineralized tissue.

The ancient DNA field — pioneered by Svante Pääbo, who won the 2022 Nobel Prize for sequencing Neanderthal genomes — has spent decades developing methods to extract and sequence these tiny, damaged fragments while distinguishing genuine ancient sequences from modern contamination.
For microbiome research specifically, the most productive substrates have turned out to be dental calculus — the mineralized plaque that accumulates on teeth — and coprolites, the preserved feces.
Dental calculus is remarkable because the calcified mineral matrix protects bacterial DNA from degradation, sometimes preserving it for thousands or even tens of thousands of years.
When archaeologists excavate a skeleton, the teeth often still carry calcified plaque from the last weeks or months of the individual's life.
Drill into that calculus, extract DNA, sequence it, and you get a snapshot of that person's oral microbiome at the time of death.
The first systematic studies of ancient oral microbiomes by Christina Warinner, Cecil Lewis, and colleagues, around 2014, established that the technique worked and produced fascinating results.

They found that the oral microbiomes of medieval Europeans were broadly recognizable — many of the same species you'd find in modern mouths — but with significant differences in composition that correlated with the agricultural transition, dietary shifts, and the introduction of refined sugar and grains.
The advent of farming, around 10,000 years ago, was associated with a shift in oral microbiomes toward more cariogenic (cavity-causing) species.
The industrial revolution and the introduction of refined sugar were associated with another major shift.
The oral microbiome carries a record of human dietary and cultural change going back to the Neolithic.

Similar work on Neanderthal dental calculus by Laura Weyrich and others has produced glimpses of Neanderthal diets and oral microbial communities — including evidence that some Neanderthals were consuming plant-rich diets with possibly medicinal use of poplar bark (a source of salicylic acid, the precursor to aspirin), and the recovery of microbial sequences from extinct lineages of oral bacteria.

Coprolites are rarer than dental calculus because preservation conditions for feces are unusual, but where they exist — particularly in arid caves and ice mummies — they yield extraordinary information about ancient gut microbiomes.

Studies of coprolites from pre-Columbian sites in the Americas have found gut microbiomes more similar to those of present-day rural, non-industrialized populations than to those of modern Western populations, supporting the broader narrative that industrialization has progressively depleted human gut microbiomes of diversity that was present throughout most of human history.

The "missing microbes" hypothesis, articulated most prominently by Martin Blaser, builds on this — the idea that modern lifestyle factors (antibiotics, processed diet, C-sections, decreased microbial exposure) have collectively eroded the human microbiome in ways that may contribute to the rise of chronic diseases of industrialization.
A particularly dramatic application of ancient microbiomics has been the recovery of ancient pathogen genomes.

Sequencing of skeletal material from medieval plague victims has produced complete genomes of the Yersinia pestis strains that caused the Black Death, allowing direct comparison with modern plague strains and detailed reconstruction of how the pathogen has evolved over centuries.
Similar work has been done on tuberculosis, leprosy, syphilis, and Spanish flu.

The ability to read the genomes of ancient pathogens turns the historical record of epidemics into something we can now study molecularly — which strains caused which outbreaks, how they spread, how they evolved in response to host immunity and changing conditions.

The methods have also been turned, more recently, on environmental samples — sediment cores from lake bottoms, permafrost cores, ice cores — to reconstruct ancient environmental microbial communities and trace how microbial ecosystems have changed in response to climate shifts, human impact, and geological events.
The Joint Genome Institute, the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology, and several other major centers have substantial ancient microbiome programs.
The take-home: the same DNA sequencing technology that lets us read living microbiomes lets us read dead ones too, going back tens of thousands of years.
The microbiome is no longer just a snapshot of contemporary biology.
It is also a historical archive, recorded in bones, teeth, frozen tissues, and preserved sediments, that we can now read with the same instruments we use for living samples.

The past microbiome is partly open to us, and what we're finding is that the modern human microbiome — at least in industrialized populations — is in important ways a depleted, simplified version of what humans carried with them for most of our evolutionary history.
